sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute sore throat is usually viral and self-limiting, but some patients have streptococcal infection or a deeper space or airway problem. Prehospital care should focus on airway assessment, hydration, analgesia, and recognition of red flags such as quinsy, epiglottitis, or neck space infection.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Throat pain, painful swallowing, fever, headache, or malaise
- Erythematous pharynx or tonsils, exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy
- Hoarse voice or coryzal symptoms in viral illness
- Drooling, trismus, stridor, muffled voice, or neck swelling in more serious disease

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Viral symptoms such as cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, or diarrhoea make bacterial pharyngitis less likely
- Clinical features alone do not reliably distinguish all bacterial from viral causes
- Red flags include airway compromise, drooling, trismus, stridor, unilateral swelling, severe dehydration, or toxic appearance

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Assess airway and hydration first
- Provide simple analgesia, fluids, and supportive care
- Warm saline gargles, lozenges, or local symptomatic measures may help if tolerated
- Escalate promptly if red flags or inability to swallow fluids are present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider stronger analgesia within scope for severe pain
- Corticosteroid or antibiotic therapy should be reserved for selected severe or bacterial-suspected cases after medical consultation or according to local protocol
- Pre-notify or transport urgently for suspected peritonsillar abscess, epiglottitis, deep neck infection, or airway compromise

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Most uncomplicated sore throats resolve within about a week without antibiotics
- Avoid routine antibiotic use in straightforward low-risk sore throat
